Algorithm Development and Speed Comparisons¶

This notebook documents the development of the worn indicator algorithm, comparing different implementation approaches:

  • Pure pandas (slow)
  • Pandas with rolling windows (slow)
  • Pure numpy (moderate)
  • Numba-compiled numpy (fast)

This is educational/development code - for production data processing, use 01_pipeline.ipynb which uses the fastest approach.

Imports¶

In [1]:
import subprocess

import altair as alt
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from tqdm import tqdm

from utils import (
    CHAR_LOOKUP,
    get_datadir,
    worn_indicator_pandas_single,
    worn_indicator_pandas_rolling,
    worn_indicator_numpy_single,
    worn_indicator_numpy,
    worn_indicator_numba_single,
    worn_indicator_numba,
)
In [2]:
pd.set_option("display.max_columns", None)
pd.set_option("display.max_rows", 1000)
alt.data_transformers.enable("vegafusion")
alt.renderers.enable('png')
tqdm.pandas()

Load raw data¶

In [3]:
year: str = "2003-2004"
In [4]:
datadir = get_datadir(year)
parquetfile = datadir / f"paxraw_{CHAR_LOOKUP[year].lower()}.parquet"

paxraw = pd.read_parquet(parquetfile)
In [5]:
paxraw.shape
Out[5]:
(72250027, 8)

Work with a sample of data¶

For development, use a single person's data to test algorithms quickly.

In [6]:
paxraw_sample = paxraw.loc[paxraw.SEQN == paxraw.SEQN.values[0], :].copy()
paxraw_sample.shape
Out[6]:
(10080, 8)
In [7]:
min_worn_hours_threshold: int = 10
max_nonzero_count_per_unworn_hour: int = 2
max_of_nonzero_in_unworn_hour: int = 100
MINUTES_PER_HOUR = 60

Approach 1: Pure pandas with iloc¶

This approach uses pandas iloc for iteration - turns out it's very slow due to pandas overhead.

In [8]:
paxraw_sample.loc[:, "worn"] = True
PAXINTEN_col = np.arange(paxraw_sample.shape[1])[paxraw_sample.columns == "PAXINTEN"][0]
worn_col = np.arange(paxraw_sample.shape[1])[paxraw_sample.columns == "worn"][0]
In [9]:
# First hour check
if (paxraw_sample.iloc[:60, PAXINTEN_col] >= 100).sum() <= 2:
    paxraw_sample.iloc[:60, worn_col] = False
In [10]:
for i in range(61, paxraw_sample.shape[0]):
    if (paxraw_sample.iloc[(i - 60) : i, PAXINTEN_col] >= 100).sum() <= 2:
        paxraw_sample.iloc[(i - 60) : i, worn_col] = False

Visualize worn indicator results¶

In [11]:
worn_chart = (
    alt.Chart(paxraw_sample)
    .mark_bar(width=1)
    .encode(x="PAXN:O", y=alt.value(-10), y2=alt.value(2), color="worn")
)
inten_chart = alt.Chart(paxraw_sample).mark_line(color="orange").encode(x="PAXN:O", y="PAXINTEN")
if "PAXSTEP" in paxraw_sample.columns:
    step_chart = alt.Chart(paxraw_sample).mark_line().encode(x="PAXN:O", y="PAXSTEP")
    chart = worn_chart + inten_chart + step_chart
else:
    chart = worn_chart + inten_chart
chart.properties(width=1400, height=600)
Out[11]:

Correct the logic¶

The above had incorrect logic. The correct definition:

  • Only 2 allowed > 0, AND all 60 are strictly less than 100
In [12]:
# Reset and apply correct logic
paxraw_sample.loc[:, "worn"] = True

# First hour
if ((paxraw_sample.iloc[:60, PAXINTEN_col] > 0).sum() <= 2) and (
    (paxraw_sample.iloc[:60, PAXINTEN_col] < 100).sum() == 60
):
    paxraw_sample.iloc[:60, worn_col] = False
In [13]:
for i in range(61, paxraw_sample.shape[0]):
    if ((paxraw_sample.iloc[(i - 60) : i, PAXINTEN_col] > 0).sum() <= 2) and (
        (paxraw_sample.iloc[(i - 60) : i, PAXINTEN_col] < 100).sum() == 60
    ):
        paxraw_sample.iloc[(i - 60) : i, worn_col] = False
In [14]:
(
    alt.Chart(paxraw_sample)
    .mark_bar(width=5, opacity=0.3)
    .encode(
        x="PAXN:O",
        y=alt.value(600),
        y2=alt.value(2),
        color=alt.Color("worn", scale=alt.Scale(range=["white", "grey"])),
    )
    + alt.Chart(paxraw_sample)
    .mark_line(color="orange", clip=True)
    .encode(x="PAXN:O", y=alt.Y("PAXINTEN", scale=alt.Scale(domain=[0, 5000])))
).properties(width=1400, height=600)
Out[14]:

Try inverse logic (start unworn, set to worn)¶

This shows the same result with inverted logic.

In [15]:
# Start with worn = False
paxraw_sample.loc[:, "worn"] = False

if ((paxraw_sample.iloc[:60, PAXINTEN_col] > 0).sum() > 2) or (
    (paxraw_sample.iloc[:60, PAXINTEN_col] >= 100).sum() > 0
):
    paxraw_sample.iloc[:60, worn_col] = True

for i in range(61, paxraw_sample.shape[0]):
    if ((paxraw_sample.iloc[(i - 60) : i, PAXINTEN_col] > 0).sum() > 2) or (
        (paxraw_sample.iloc[(i - 60) : i, PAXINTEN_col] >= 100).sum() > 0
    ):
        paxraw_sample.iloc[(i - worn_col) : i, worn_col] = True
In [16]:
(
    alt.Chart(paxraw_sample)
    .mark_bar(width=5, opacity=0.3)
    .encode(
        x="PAXN:O",
        y=alt.value(600),
        y2=alt.value(2),
        color=alt.Color("worn", scale=alt.Scale(range=["white", "grey"])),
    )
    + alt.Chart(paxraw_sample)
    .mark_line(color="orange", clip=True)
    .encode(x="PAXN:O", y=alt.Y("PAXINTEN", scale=alt.Scale(domain=[0, 5000])))
).properties(width=1400, height=600)
Out[16]:

Approach 2: Simple numpy array loop¶

A straightforward implementation using numpy arrays and a for loop.

In [17]:
# Initialize worn indicator
worn = np.ones(paxraw_sample.shape[0])
paxinten = paxraw_sample.PAXINTEN.values
In [18]:
# Check first hour
if ((paxinten[:MINUTES_PER_HOUR] > 0).sum() <= max_nonzero_count_per_unworn_hour) and (
    (paxinten[:MINUTES_PER_HOUR] < max_of_nonzero_in_unworn_hour).sum() == MINUTES_PER_HOUR
):
    worn[:MINUTES_PER_HOUR] = 0
In [19]:
# Main loop
for i in range(MINUTES_PER_HOUR + 1, worn.shape[0]):
    if ((paxinten[(i - MINUTES_PER_HOUR) : i] > 0).sum() <= max_nonzero_count_per_unworn_hour) and (
        (paxinten[(i - MINUTES_PER_HOUR) : i] < max_of_nonzero_in_unworn_hour).sum()
        == MINUTES_PER_HOUR
    ):
        worn[(i - MINUTES_PER_HOUR) : i] = 0

Approach 3: Use numba and apply to full dataset¶

This is faster on the single person data and also a tad faster when doing the "looping" over people using numba directly (passing the whole vector of values for the full df to numba, rather than per-person).

Since it's @jit compiled, the numba versions are in the utils.py folder here. We've also written the implementations of the other approaches in the utils script so we can compare them.

Timing Comparisons¶

Compare all approaches on a single person's data.

In [20]:
# Compile numba function first (so compilation time isn't included)
_ = worn_indicator_numba_single(paxraw_sample.PAXINTEN.values)
In [21]:
# Time all single-person approaches
t_pandas_iloc = %timeit -o -q worn_indicator_pandas_single(paxraw_sample.PAXINTEN.values)
t_pandas_rolling = %timeit -o -q worn_indicator_pandas_rolling(paxraw_sample.PAXINTEN.values)
t_numpy = %timeit -o -q worn_indicator_numpy_single(paxraw_sample.PAXINTEN.values)
t_numba = %timeit -o -q worn_indicator_numba_single(paxraw_sample.PAXINTEN.values)
In [22]:
# Build comparison table
n_people = paxraw["SEQN"].nunique()

def format_time(seconds):
    if seconds >= 1:
        return f"{seconds:.2f}s"
    elif seconds >= 0.001:
        return f"{seconds * 1000:.1f}ms"
    else:
        return f"{seconds * 1_000_000:.1f}μs"

timing_results = pd.DataFrame({
    "Approach": ["Pandas iloc", "Pandas rolling", "Numpy loop", "Numba"],
    "Function": [
        "worn_indicator_pandas_single",
        "worn_indicator_pandas_rolling",
        "worn_indicator_numpy_single",
        "worn_indicator_numba_single",
    ],
    "Time per person": [format_time(t.average) for t in [t_pandas_iloc, t_pandas_rolling, t_numpy, t_numba]],
    "Projected total": [format_time(t.average * n_people) for t in [t_pandas_iloc, t_pandas_rolling, t_numpy, t_numba]],
})
timing_results
Out[22]:
Approach Function Time per person Projected total
0 Pandas iloc worn_indicator_pandas_single 836.6ms 6003.71s
1 Pandas rolling worn_indicator_pandas_rolling 407.4ms 2923.82s
2 Numpy loop worn_indicator_numpy_single 22.1ms 158.25s
3 Numba worn_indicator_numba_single 704.1μs 5.05s

Why we don't use slow approaches¶

DON'T: Apply numpy-based function to Pandas column¶

This would take ~25 minutes:

paxraw['worn'] = 1
for SEQN in tqdm(pd.unique(paxraw.SEQN.values)):
    paxraw.loc[paxraw.SEQN == SEQN, 'worn'] = worn_indicator_numpy_single(paxraw.loc[paxraw.SEQN == SEQN, 'PAXINTEN'].values)

DON'T: Pandas iloc or rolling approaches¶

These are even slower per person:

  • worn_indicator_pandas_single - ~1s per person (~8+ hours total)
  • worn_indicator_pandas_rolling - slightly faster but still too slow

Full Dataset vs Per-Person Loop¶

Comparing the actual overhead of applying functions per-person (with pandas filtering) vs operating on the full dataset directly.

In [23]:
# Test on 10 people to see the pandas overhead
n_test = 10
paxraw_subset = paxraw.loc[paxraw.SEQN.isin(paxraw.SEQN.unique()[:n_test]), :].copy()
In [36]:
# First, show that different looping strategies have similar overhead
def apply_loop_loc_twice(df, func):
    """Loop with .loc called twice per iteration (original approach)."""
    df = df.copy()
    df['worn'] = 1.0
    for SEQN in df.SEQN.unique():
        df.loc[df.SEQN == SEQN, 'worn'] = func(df.loc[df.SEQN == SEQN, 'PAXINTEN'].values)
    return df

def apply_loop_mask(df, func):
    """Loop with pre-computed mask."""
    df = df.copy()
    df['worn'] = 1.0
    for SEQN in df.SEQN.unique():
        mask = df.SEQN == SEQN
        df.loc[mask, 'worn'] = func(df.loc[mask, 'PAXINTEN'].values)
    return df

def apply_groupby(df, func):
    """Groupby transform."""
    df = df.copy()
    df['worn'] = df.groupby('SEQN')['PAXINTEN'].transform(lambda x: func(x.values))
    return df

# Compare looping strategies using numba_single as the function
t_loc_twice = %timeit -o -q -n1 -r3 apply_loop_loc_twice(paxraw_subset, worn_indicator_numba_single)
t_mask = %timeit -o -q -n1 -r3 apply_loop_mask(paxraw_subset, worn_indicator_numba_single)
t_groupby = %timeit -o -q -n1 -r3 apply_groupby(paxraw_subset, worn_indicator_numba_single)

looping_strategies = pd.DataFrame({
    "Looping Strategy": ["Loop .loc twice", "Loop with mask", "Groupby transform"],
    f"Time ({n_test} people)": [format_time(t.average) for t in [t_loc_twice, t_mask, t_groupby]],
})
print("All looping strategies have similar overhead (using numba_single):")
looping_strategies

All looping strategies have similar overhead (using numba_single):
Out[36]:
Looping Strategy Time (10 people)
0 Loop .loc twice 18.1ms
1 Loop with mask 11.8ms
2 Groupby transform 9.8ms
In [34]:

t_loc_twice = %timeit -o -q -n1 -r3 apply_loop_loc_twice(paxraw_subset, worn_indicator_pandas_rolling)
t_mask = %timeit -o -q -n1 -r3 apply_loop_mask(paxraw_subset, worn_indicator_pandas_rolling)
t_groupby = %timeit -o -q -n1 -r3 apply_groupby(paxraw_subset, worn_indicator_pandas_rolling)

looping_strategies = pd.DataFrame({
    "Looping Strategy": ["Loop .loc twice", "Loop with mask", "Groupby transform"],
    f"Time ({n_test} people)": [format_time(t.average) for t in [t_loc_twice, t_mask, t_groupby]],
})
print("All looping strategies have similar overhead (using pandas_rolling):")
looping_strategies

In [33]:
# Compare looping strategies using numba_single as the function
t_loc_twice = %timeit -o -q -n1 -r3 apply_loop_loc_twice(paxraw_subset, worn_indicator_numpy_single)
t_mask = %timeit -o -q -n1 -r3 apply_loop_mask(paxraw_subset, worn_indicator_numpy_single)
t_groupby = %timeit -o -q -n1 -r3 apply_groupby(paxraw_subset, worn_indicator_numpy_single)

looping_strategies = pd.DataFrame({
    "Looping Strategy": ["Loop .loc twice", "Loop with mask", "Groupby transform"],
    f"Time ({n_test} people)": [format_time(t.average) for t in [t_loc_twice, t_mask, t_groupby]],
})

All looping strategies have similar overhead (using numpy_single):
Out[33]:
Looping Strategy Time (10 people)
0 Loop .loc twice 205.4ms
1 Loop with mask 200.9ms
2 Groupby transform 199.5ms
In [29]:
def apply_via_loop(df, func):
    """Apply a single-person function via loop with mask."""
    df = df.copy()
    df['worn'] = 1.0
    for SEQN in df.SEQN.unique():
        mask = df.SEQN == SEQN
        df.loc[mask, 'worn'] = func(df.loc[mask, 'PAXINTEN'].values)
    return df

def apply_full_dataset(df):
    """Apply worn_indicator_numba to full dataset - fastest."""
    df = df.copy()
    df['worn'] = worn_indicator_numba(df.PAXINTEN.values, df.SEQN.values)
    return df

# Time all single-person functions applied via loop
t_pandas_iloc_loop = %timeit -o -q -n1 -r1 apply_via_loop(paxraw_subset, worn_indicator_pandas_single)
t_pandas_rolling_loop = %timeit -o -q -n1 -r1 apply_via_loop(paxraw_subset, worn_indicator_pandas_rolling)
t_numpy_loop = %timeit -o -q -n1 -r1 apply_via_loop(paxraw_subset, worn_indicator_numpy_single)
t_numba_loop = %timeit -o -q -n1 -r3 apply_via_loop(paxraw_subset, worn_indicator_numba_single)

# Time full-dataset approach on subset
t_full_subset = %timeit -o -q -n1 -r3 apply_full_dataset(paxraw_subset)
In [37]:
# Compare all approaches applied via loop vs full dataset
loop_comparison = pd.DataFrame({
    "Approach": [
        "Pandas iloc (via loop)",
        "Pandas rolling (via loop)",
        "Numpy (via loop)",
        "Numba (via loop)",
        "Numba (full dataset)",
    ],
    "Function": [
        "worn_indicator_pandas_single",
        "worn_indicator_pandas_rolling",
        "worn_indicator_numpy_single",
        "worn_indicator_numba_single",
        "worn_indicator_numba",
    ],
    f"Time ({n_test} people)": [
        format_time(t_pandas_iloc_loop.average),
        format_time(t_pandas_rolling_loop.average),
        format_time(t_numpy_loop.average),
        format_time(t_numba_loop.average),
        format_time(t_full_subset.average),
    ],
    "Projected total": [
        format_time(t_pandas_iloc_loop.average / n_test * n_people),
        format_time(t_pandas_rolling_loop.average / n_test * n_people),
        format_time(t_numpy_loop.average / n_test * n_people),
        format_time(t_numba_loop.average / n_test * n_people),
        format_time(t_full_subset.average / n_test * n_people),
    ],
})
loop_comparison
Out[37]:
Approach Function Time (10 people) Projected total
0 Pandas iloc (via loop) worn_indicator_pandas_single 7.11s 5104.27s
1 Pandas rolling (via loop) worn_indicator_pandas_rolling 4.08s 2927.13s
2 Numpy (via loop) worn_indicator_numpy_single 204.3ms 146.59s
3 Numba (via loop) worn_indicator_numba_single 11.8ms 8.45s
4 Numba (full dataset) worn_indicator_numba 7.8ms 5.59s
In [31]:
# Time full-dataset approaches (these are slower, so fewer runs)
t_numpy_full = %timeit -o -q -n1 -r1 worn_indicator_numpy(paxraw.PAXINTEN.values, paxraw.SEQN.values)
t_numba_full = %timeit -o -q -n1 -r3 worn_indicator_numba(paxraw.PAXINTEN.values, paxraw.SEQN.values)

print(f"Numpy full dataset ({n_people:,} people, {len(paxraw):,} rows): {format_time(t_numpy_full.average)}")
print(f"Numba full dataset ({n_people:,} people, {len(paxraw):,} rows): {format_time(t_numba_full.average)}")

Numpy full dataset (7,176 people, 72,250,027 rows): 156.67s
Numba full dataset (7,176 people, 72,250,027 rows): 5.28s

Summary¶

Key insights:

  1. Avoid pandas iloc/loc in loops - the overhead is massive
  2. Numba compilation gives ~30x speedup over numpy loops
  3. Operating on the full array (with SEQN checks) avoids per-person overhead

Conclusion: Use worn_indicator_numba for production.

In [38]:
# Final comparison - using loop timings for accurate totals
final_results = pd.DataFrame({
    "Approach": [
        "Pandas iloc (via loop)",
        "Pandas rolling (via loop)",
        "Numpy (via loop)",
        "Numba (via loop)",
        "Numpy (full dataset)",
        "Numba (full dataset)",
    ],
    "Function": [
        "worn_indicator_pandas_single",
        "worn_indicator_pandas_rolling",
        "worn_indicator_numpy_single",
        "worn_indicator_numba_single",
        "worn_indicator_numpy",
        "worn_indicator_numba",
    ],
    "Time per person": [
        format_time(t_pandas_iloc.average),
        format_time(t_pandas_rolling.average),
        format_time(t_numpy.average),
        format_time(t_numba.average),
        format_time(t_numpy_full.average / n_people),
        format_time(t_numba_full.average / n_people),
    ],
    "Total time (with overhead)": [
        format_time(t_pandas_iloc_loop.average / n_test * n_people),
        format_time(t_pandas_rolling_loop.average / n_test * n_people),
        format_time(t_numpy_loop.average / n_test * n_people),
        format_time(t_numba_loop.average / n_test * n_people),
        format_time(t_numpy_full.average),
        format_time(t_numba_full.average),
    ],
})
final_results
